skill.name: animejs
skill.description: Anime.js adapter patterns for HyperFrames. Use when writing Anime.js animations or timelines inside HyperFrames compositions, registering animations on window.__hfAnime, making Anime.js seek-driven and deterministic, or translating Anime.js examples into render-safe HyperFrames HTML.
skill.summary: Anime.js adapter for creating seek-driven animations in HyperFrames compositions.
skill.author: heygen-com

security_audit.summary: All 28 static analysis findings are false positives. The 22 'backtick execution' findings are markdown formatting (inline code and code block fences), not Ruby or shell commands. The 3 'hardcoded URL' findings are CDN library URLs and documentation references inside code examples. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' finding on line 3 is a pattern-matching error against YAML frontmatter description text. The 2 'system reconnaissance' findings target instructional text about animation best practices. The SKILL.md file contains only documentation and code examples; no executable code, no malicious patterns, and no prompt injection attempts detected.

content.value_statement: Writing Anime.js animations for video rendering requires deterministic, seek-driven instances. This skill provides the patterns and validation steps needed to make any Anime.js animation render-safe inside HyperFrames compositions.
actual_capabilities: Creates seek-driven Anime.js instances registered on window.__hfAnime for frame-accurate video rendering.
Converts standard Anime.js examples into render-safe HyperFrames HTML with autoplay disabled.
Supports both individual animation objects and timeline-based sequencing with finite durations.
Works with IIFE script tags and ES module builds loaded from CDN.
Applies best practices to avoid wall-clock dependencies and unseeded randomness.
limitations: Cannot use Anime.js callbacks that mutate the DOM based on wall-clock time or network state.
Does not support infinite loops or non-deterministic animations without finite repeat counts.
Anime.js is not the primary animation path for HyperFrames; GSAP covers complex scene sequencing.
The skill provides authoring guidance only; it does not install or configure Anime.js dependencies.

use_cases.title: Adding decorative SVG animations to a video composition
use_cases.description: A motion designer adds subtle SVG flourishes to a title card using the compact Anime.js syntax.

use_cases.title: Porting an existing Anime.js animation to video
use_cases.description: A developer takes working Anime.js animation code from a web project and makes it seek-driven for video rendering.
use_cases.target_user: Web developers migrating browser animations to video

use_cases.title: Building micro-animations for UI elements in a product demo
use_cases.description: A content creator needs multiple independent micro-animations for UI elements in a product walkthrough video.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators producing product demo videos

prompt_templates.title: Basic seek-driven animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a first Anime.js animation for HyperFrames
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Anime.js animation that moves a box across the screen. Set autoplay to false and register it on window.__hfAnime. Use a finite duration and easing function.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-step timeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a sequenced animation with multiple elements
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an Anime.js timeline with three steps. First fade in a title, then slide in a subtitle, then scale up a call-to-action button. Each step should have a different duration and easing. Register the timeline on window.__hfAnime.

prompt_templates.title: Module import pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Using Anime.js ES module build in HyperFrames
prompt_templates.prompt: Write HyperFrames HTML that imports Anime.js from CDN using ES module syntax. Create an animation with autoplay disabled and push it to the __hfAnime registry. Use the animate function from the module build.

prompt_templates.title: Multiple independent micro-animations
prompt_templates.scenario: Coordinating several independent animations in one composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three independent Anime.js micro-animations in a single HyperFrames composition. Each targets different elements with different properties, easing functions, and durations. Push all three to the __hfAnime array.

output_examples.input: Animate a circle scaling up and changing color over 1 second.
output_examples.output: A single HTML file with an Anime.js script that creates a seek-driven animation. It targets a circle element, scales from 0 to 1, changes fill color from blue to red over 1000ms with easeOutQuad easing, and registers the animation on window.__hfAnime.

output_examples.input: Create a staggered entrance for three elements.
output_examples.output: An HTML composition using an Anime.js timeline. Three elements appear in sequence with 200ms offset between each. The first fades in, the second slides up, and the third rotates into place. Each animation has a finite duration and the timeline is registered for HyperFrames seeking.

output_examples.input: Add an Anime.js animation using ES module imports.
output_examples.output: A HyperFrames-compatible HTML file using script type=module to import animate from the Anime.js CDN. The animation creates a pulsing glow effect with opacity and scale transforms, uses autoplay false, and registers the instance.

best_practices: Always set autoplay to false so HyperFrames controls the animation clock.
Use finite durations and loop counts to ensure animations complete within the composition time.
Register every animation or timeline with window.__hfAnime.push() instead of relying on auto-discovery.
anti_patterns: Leaving autoplay at the Anime.js default causes timeline drift and desynchronization during video rendering.
Building animations inside timers, promises, or event handlers breaks seek-driven determinism.
Using anime.running for auto-discovery instead of explicit registration misses animations that HyperFrames needs to track.

faq.question: Why must autoplay be set to false?
faq.answer: HyperFrames controls the animation clock for frame-accurate seeking. Autoplay would let Anime.js advance on its own clock, causing desynchronization.

faq.question: Can I use Anime.js callback functions?
faq.answer: Avoid callbacks that mutate the DOM based on wall-clock time, network state, or unseeded randomness. They break determinism needed for video rendering.

faq.question: Does this work with Anime.js module builds?
faq.answer: Yes. The adapter works with both IIFE and ES module builds. It only needs the returned object to expose seek, pause, and play methods.

faq.question: How do I register multiple animations?
faq.answer: Push each animation or timeline to the window.__hfAnime array. The adapter seeks all registered instances at each frame.

faq.question: Can I use infinite loops in Anime.js for HyperFrames?
faq.answer: No. Compute a finite repeat count from the composition duration. Infinite loops prevent the animation from completing.

faq.question: Should I use Anime.js or GSAP for my composition?
faq.answer: GSAP is the primary HyperFrames authoring path for complex scene sequencing. Use Anime.js for small SVG and DOM flourishes where Anime.js syntax is more compact.
